title: Resolve, combine, reconstruct: vectors and components

objectives.statement: Distinguish scalars from vectors and represent two- and three-dimensional vectors using signed components, coordinates, and unit-vector notation.
Add, subtract, and scale vectors componentwise and interpret the resulting direction in a stated coordinate convention.
Resolve magnitude-and-direction descriptions into components and reconstruct magnitude and direction with quadrant-aware checks.
Use unit vectors and the dot product to test alignment, recover an angle, and calculate a scalar projection.

scenes.title: Direction survives the arithmetic
scenes.contentHtml:
Direction survives the arithmetic

A fictional route moves 8 units east and then 6 units north. One report calls the net change 14 units because 8+6=148+6=14.

The two legs total 14 units, but the vector from start to finish is not found by adding their lengths. With east as positive horizontal and north as positive vertical, its components are (8,6)(8,6) and its magnitude is

82+62=10 units.\sqrt{8^2+6^2}=10\text{ units}.

Its direction is atan2⁡(6,8)≈36.87∘\operatorname{atan2}(6,8)\approx36.87^\circ north of east. The direction did not disappear when the two legs were combined.

This lesson uses a four-part vector audit: resolve every vector into signed components, combine matching components, reconstruct magnitude and direction, then check signs, units, and bounds.

Starting check

  • Is temperature alone a scalar or a vector?
  • What sign should a westward horizontal component have if east is positive?
  • Can two nonzero vectors add to the zero vector?

If a spatial description is difficult, replace it with an axis convention and a component table before calculating.

Source Note
claims claim-scalar-vector, claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-direction-componentssources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

NIST DLMF records the scalar/vector, component, norm, and angle relations used here. The route and audit are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every essential vector is given through words, signed component pairs, coordinates, and equations. No task requires seeing an arrow, estimating direction visually, using color, or dragging an object.

scenes.title: Represent vectors with signed components
scenes.contentHtml:
Represent vectors with signed components

In this lesson, a scalar is one real numerical value. It may be positive, zero, or negative and has no vector direction. A vector is a mathematical object that can be added to another vector and multiplied by scalars; once Cartesian axes are declared, a vector 𝐯\mathbf v can be written as

𝐯=(vx,vy,vz)=vx𝐢+vy𝐣+vz𝐤,\mathbf v=(v_x,v_y,v_z)=v_x\mathbf i+v_y\mathbf j+v_z\mathbf k,

where 𝐢,𝐣,𝐤\mathbf i,\mathbf j,\mathbf k are unit vectors along the positive axes. Components are signed: a negative vxv_x points opposite the positive horizontal direction. Every Euclidean vector has a nonnegative magnitude, but only a nonzero vector determines a unique direction.

Arithmetic and magnitude

For 𝐮=(ux,uy,uz)\mathbf u=(u_x,u_y,u_z) and 𝐯=(vx,vy,vz)\mathbf v=(v_x,v_y,v_z),

𝐮±𝐯=(ux±vx,uy±vy,uz±vz),\mathbf u\mathbin{\pm}\mathbf v=(u_x\mathbin{\pm}v_x,u_y\mathbin{\pm}v_y,u_z\mathbin{\pm}v_z),
c𝐯=(cvx,cvy,cvz),‖𝐯‖=vx2+vy2+vz2.c\mathbf v=(cv_x,cv_y,cv_z),\qquad \lVert\mathbf v\rVert=\sqrt{v_x^2+v_y^2+v_z^2}.

The two-dimensional forms use the same rules with the zz components omitted.

For nonzero 𝐯\mathbf v, its direction unit vector is 𝐯^=𝐯/‖𝐯‖\widehat{\mathbf v}=\mathbf v/\lVert\mathbf v\rVert. The zero vector has magnitude zero but no unique direction, so it cannot be normalized.

Resolve and reconstruct

Declare that θ\theta is measured counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis. A vector with magnitude rr then has

vx=rcos⁡θ,vy=rsin⁡θ.v_x=r\cos\theta,\qquad v_y=r\sin\theta.

Reconstruct with r=vx2+vy2r=\sqrt{v_x^2+v_y^2} and a quadrant-aware two-argument inverse tangent, θ=atan2⁡(vy,vx)\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(v_y,v_x). The vertical component is the first argument and the horizontal component is the second, so both signs are available for choosing the quadrant. For directions reported from 0∘0^\circ through 360∘360^\circ, start with the principal value of arctan⁡(vy/vx)\arctan(v_y/v_x): add 180∘180^\circ when vx<0v_x<0, and add 360∘360^\circ when vx>0v_x>0 but the principal value is negative. If vx=0v_x=0, the direction is 90∘90^\circ for vy>0v_y>0 or 270∘270^\circ for vy<0v_y<0. The zero vector has no direction. The two-argument function performs these quadrant choices directly.

Dot product

𝐮⋅𝐯=uxvx+uyvy+uzvz=‖𝐮‖‖𝐯‖cos⁡ϕ.\mathbf u\mathbin{\boldsymbol\cdot}\mathbf v=u_xv_x+u_yv_y+u_zv_z =\lVert\mathbf u\rVert\lVert\mathbf v\rVert\cos\phi.

In two dimensions, omit the zz term.

The sign describes alignment: positive for an acute included angle, zero for perpendicular nonzero vectors, and negative for an obtuse angle. If 𝐝^\widehat{\mathbf d} is a unit direction, 𝐯⋅𝐝^\mathbf v\mathbin{\boldsymbol\cdot}\widehat{\mathbf d} is the signed scalar projection of 𝐯\mathbf v along it.

Source Note
claims claim-scalar-vector, claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-dot-product, claim-direction-componentssources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

NIST DLMF supports the scalar/vector boundary and the component, unit-vector, scalar-product, norm, and angle relations used here. The resolve-combine-reconstruct-check organization is original synthesis.

scenes.title: Resolve and reconstruct a directed span
scenes.contentHtml:
Resolve and reconstruct a directed span

A fictional directed span has magnitude 20 units at 140∘140^\circ, measured counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis. Resolve it, normalize it, and reconstruct its magnitude and direction.

Worked Example
id worked-example-directed-span

1. Predict signs. An angle of 140∘140^\circ is in quadrant II, so the horizontal component must be negative and the vertical component positive.

2. Resolve.

vx=20cos⁡140∘≈−15.321,v_x=20\cos140^\circ\approx-15.321,
vy=20sin⁡140∘≈12.856.v_y=20\sin140^\circ\approx12.856.

Thus 𝐯≈(−15.321,12.856)\mathbf v\approx(-15.321,12.856) units.

3. Normalize. Divide by magnitude 20:

𝐯^≈(−0.7660,0.6428).\widehat{\mathbf v}\approx(-0.7660,0.6428).

4. Reconstruct magnitude.

(−15.321)2+(12.856)2≈20.\sqrt{(-15.321)^2+(12.856)^2}\approx20.

5. Reconstruct direction.

atan2⁡(12.856,−15.321)≈140∘.\operatorname{atan2}(12.856,-15.321)\approx140^\circ.

6. Check. The unit-vector components have squared sum about 1; the signs match quadrant II; neither component magnitude exceeds the vector magnitude.

Use a dot product and a scalar projection

Let 𝐩=(2,3)\mathbf p=(2,3) and 𝐪=(−1,4)\mathbf q=(-1,4). Their dot product is

𝐩⋅𝐪=2(−1)+3(4)=10.\mathbf p\mathbin{\boldsymbol\cdot}\mathbf q=2(-1)+3(4)=10.

Because the result is positive, the included angle is acute. More precisely,

cos⁡ϕ=101317,ϕ≈47.7∘.\cos\phi=\frac{10}{\sqrt{13}\sqrt{17}},\qquad \phi\approx47.7^\circ.

For the unit direction 𝐝^=(3/5,4/5)\widehat{\mathbf d}=(3/5,4/5), first check that (3/5)2+(4/5)2=1(3/5)^2+(4/5)^2=1. The signed scalar projection of 𝐩\mathbf p along that direction is

𝐩⋅𝐝^=65+125=185=3.6.\mathbf p\mathbin{\boldsymbol\cdot}\widehat{\mathbf d}=\frac{6}{5}+\frac{12}{5}=\frac{18}{5}=3.6.

The positive sign means that 𝐩\mathbf p has a component along 𝐝^\widehat{\mathbf d} rather than opposite it.

Check
id check-quadrant-aware-direction

Why is arctan⁡(12.856/−15.321)≈−40∘\arctan(12.856/-15.321)\approx-40^\circ not the final direction here?

The ratio alone cannot distinguish quadrant II from quadrant IV. Because the horizontal component is negative, add 180∘180^\circ to the principal value: −40∘+180∘=140∘-40^\circ+180^\circ=140^\circ.

Source Note
claims claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-dot-product, claim-direction-componentssources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

The directed span, values, solution, and checks are original applications of the sourced relations.

scenes.title: Add components, not magnitudes
scenes.contentHtml:
Add components, not magnitudes

Misconception
id misconception-add-magnitudes

1. Predict. Two moves are 𝐮=(3,0)\mathbf u=(3,0) and 𝐯=(0,4)\mathbf v=(0,4). Before continuing, commit to a prediction: is the magnitude of 𝐮+𝐯\mathbf u+\mathbf v equal to 3+4=73+4=7?

2. Name the tempting model. The incorrect shortcut is “add the magnitudes to get the magnitude of the sum.” It ignores the directions encoded by the components.

3. Test it. Component addition gives 𝐮+𝐯=(3,4)\mathbf u+\mathbf v=(3,4), whose magnitude is 32+42=5\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5, not 7. An even sharper counterexample uses 𝐮=(3,0)\mathbf u=(3,0) and 𝐯=(−3,0)\mathbf v=(-3,0): both magnitudes are 3, but

𝐮+𝐯=(0,0),‖𝐮+𝐯‖=0.\mathbf u+\mathbf v=(0,0),\qquad\lVert\mathbf u+\mathbf v\rVert=0.

4. Explain the failure. A magnitude is nonnegative, so it discards the signs that distinguish reinforcement, partial cancellation, and complete cancellation.

5. Repair the model. Resolve first, add corresponding signed components, and only then reconstruct the magnitude. The triangle inequality

‖𝐮+𝐯‖≤‖𝐮‖+‖𝐯‖\lVert\mathbf u+\mathbf v\rVert\leq\lVert\mathbf u\rVert+\lVert\mathbf v\rVert

is a check, not a universal equality. For two nonzero Euclidean vectors, equality holds exactly when one is a positive scalar multiple of the other, so they point in the same direction. Equality also holds if either vector is zero, even though the zero vector has no direction.

6. Recheck. For 𝐚=(2,0)\mathbf a=(2,0) and 𝐛=(0,2)\mathbf b=(0,2), decide whether the resultant magnitude is 4 before reading the feedback.

Feedback: 𝐚+𝐛=(2,2)\mathbf a+\mathbf b=(2,2) and ‖𝐚+𝐛‖=22\lVert\mathbf a+\mathbf b\rVert=2\sqrt{2}, so the sum-of-magnitudes shortcut fails again.

Source Note
claims claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-triangle-inequalitysources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

The prediction sequence, counterexamples, and repair are original teaching expression based on standard component arithmetic, norms, and the triangle inequality.

scenes.title: Choose signs before calculating
scenes.contentHtml:
Choose signs before calculating

Pause at each prompt and write or say an answer before continuing to its feedback. In print, cover the feedback if useful; with a screen reader, stop after the prompt and resume only after committing to an answer.

1. Add and scale

Let 𝐚=(2,−5)\mathbf a=(2,-5) and 𝐛=(−7,3)\mathbf b=(-7,3). Find 2𝐚+𝐛2\mathbf a+\mathbf b.

Feedback: 2𝐚+𝐛=(4,−10)+(−7,3)=(−3,−7)2\mathbf a+\mathbf b=(4,-10)+(-7,3)=(-3,-7).

2. Normalize

Find a unit vector in the direction of (6,8)(6,8).

Feedback: The magnitude is 10, so the unit vector is (0.6,0.8)(0.6,0.8). Its magnitude is 1.

3. Test perpendicularity

Are (3,4)(3,4) and (−4,3)(-4,3) perpendicular?

Feedback: Their dot product is 3(−4)+4(3)=03(-4)+4(3)=0. Because both are nonzero, they are perpendicular.

4. Project onto a direction

Let 𝐯=(6,2)\mathbf v=(6,2) and let 𝐝^=(3/5,4/5)\widehat{\mathbf d}=(3/5,4/5). Find the signed scalar projection of 𝐯\mathbf v along 𝐝^\widehat{\mathbf d}.

Feedback: 𝐯⋅𝐝^=18/5+8/5=26/5=5.2\mathbf v\mathbin{\boldsymbol\cdot}\widehat{\mathbf d}=18/5+8/5=26/5=5.2 units. Check first that 𝐝^\widehat{\mathbf d} has magnitude 1.

Recovery route

  • Wrong resultant: align vectors by axis and add matching signed components.
  • Wrong quadrant: predict component signs before using trigonometry.
  • Unit vector does not have magnitude 1: divide every component by the original magnitude.
  • Dot product returned a vector: multiply corresponding components and add to obtain one scalar.
  • Angle formula fails: check for a zero vector before dividing by magnitudes.
Source Note
claims claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-dot-productsources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

All practice, feedback, and recovery guidance are original.

scenes.title: Audit a changed angle convention
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit a changed angle convention

A fictional imaging-stage calibration records vector 𝐚\mathbf a as 12 units at 30∘30^\circ clockwise from the positive vertical axis. Its component system uses positive horizontal to the right and positive vertical upward. A draft calculation treated the angle as if it were measured counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis. After 𝐚\mathbf a, the stage applies 𝐛=(−4,7)\mathbf b=(-4,7) units. Audit the convention error, then find the correct resultant vector, magnitude, and standard direction measured counterclockwise from positive horizontal.

Feedback after a complete attempt

Changing the reference axis changes which trigonometric function gives each component. Here,

𝐚=(12sin⁡30∘,12cos⁡30∘)≈(6,10.392).\mathbf a=(12\sin30^\circ,12\cos30^\circ)\approx(6,10.392).

The draft pair (10.392,6)(10.392,6) has the expected positive signs, so a sign check alone would not expose the swapped angle convention.

Combine componentwise:

𝐫=𝐚+𝐛≈(2,17.392).\mathbf r=\mathbf a+\mathbf b\approx(2,17.392).

Reconstruct:

‖𝐫‖≈22+17.3922≈17.507 units,\lVert\mathbf r\rVert\approx\sqrt{2^2+17.392^2}\approx17.507\text{ units},
θ=atan2⁡(17.392,2)≈83.44∘.\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(17.392,2)\approx83.44^\circ.

Both resultant components are positive, so quadrant I is consistent. The magnitude is below 12+65≈20.0612+\sqrt{65}\approx20.06, satisfying the triangle bound. Units remain units, not square units. The transferable rule is to project onto the declared axes rather than attach cosine permanently to the horizontal component.

The calibration setting is fictional and is not equipment guidance.

Source Note
claims claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-direction-components, claim-triangle-inequalitysources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

The calibration setting, convention error, numbers, reconstruction, and boundary statement are original.

scenes.title: Rebuild the vector audit
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the vector audit

  1. Declare axes, positive directions, angle reference, and units.
  2. Resolve every vector into signed components.
  3. Combine corresponding components; do not add magnitudes unless direction justifies it.
  4. Reconstruct magnitude with the Euclidean norm and direction with a quadrant-aware inverse tangent.
  5. Check signs, units, normalization, triangle bounds, and dot-product meaning.

Retrieval

  • How does a scalar differ from a vector?
  • Why is the zero vector impossible to normalize?
  • What does the sign of a dot product reveal?
  • Why can one-argument arctangent give the wrong direction?

Complete assessment.json. If component signs fail, return to the axis convention. If magnitude fails, redo component arithmetic before taking the norm. If a sketch feels essential, replace it with a component table and named positive directions before retrying.

Source Note
claims claim-scalar-vector, claim-vector-components, claim-vector-norm, claim-dot-product, claim-direction-components, claim-triangle-inequalitysources source-nist-dlmf-vectors

The synthesis applies sourced vector relations through an original resolve-combine-reconstruct-check method.

authors.displayName: VSBDev — accountable author with Codex assistance

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. OpenAI Codex, model gpt-5.6-sol, authored, researched, edited, and packaged this lesson in run b5cd8128-08e9-4703-b010-6a2c9517860b. The digest covers discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

items.prompt: Given u = (4,-2,5) and v = (-1,6,3), calculate 2u - v and state what each entry represents.

answer.correct: 2u - v = (8,-4,10) - (-1,6,3) = (9,-10,7). Each entry is the signed component along the corresponding declared coordinate axis.
answer.summary: (9,-10,7), with entries interpreted as signed axis components.
answer.reasoning: Vector scaling and subtraction are performed component by component.
commonErrors: Ignoring the negative first component of v

items.prompt: For w = (-2,3,6), show a sum-of-squares norm calculation, find its magnitude and a unit vector in its direction, and include a normalization check.

answer.correct: The magnitude is sqrt(4+9+36) = 7. A unit vector is (-2/7,3/7,6/7). Its squared component sum is (4+9+36)/49 = 1.
answer.reasoning: The Euclidean norm is the square root of the squared-component sum; division by that norm produces magnitude one.
commonErrors: Using the coincidentally equal signed component sum -2+3+6=7 without a sum-of-squares norm calculation

items.prompt: A vector has magnitude 10 at 210 degrees counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis. Resolve it into components and verify the quadrant and reconstructed magnitude.

answer.correct: Components are (10cos210°,10sin210°) = (-5sqrt(3),-5), approximately (-8.660,-5). Both signs are negative, matching quadrant III. The reconstructed magnitude is sqrt(75+25) = 10.
answer.reasoning: Cosine and sine give horizontal and vertical components under the stated convention.
acceptableResponses: Decimal components within 0.01 with both checks
commonErrors: Reporting the angle in the wrong quadrant

items.prompt: For a = (1,2) and b = (2,-1), calculate the dot product and interpret the included angle.

answer.correct: a dot b = 1(2)+2(-1)=0. Both vectors are nonzero, so their included angle is 90 degrees and they are perpendicular.
answer.summary: Dot product 0; the vectors are perpendicular.
answer.reasoning: For nonzero Euclidean vectors, a zero dot product makes cosine of the included angle zero.
commonErrors: Calling zero evidence of parallel vectors
Failing to note that both vectors are nonzero

items.prompt: Let v = (4,-1) and unit direction d-hat = (3/5,4/5). Find the signed scalar projection of v along d-hat and interpret its sign.

answer.correct: v dot d-hat = 12/5 - 4/5 = 8/5 = 1.6. The positive sign means v has a component in the d-hat direction rather than opposite it.
answer.reasoning: Dotting with a unit direction returns signed length along that direction.
commonErrors: Dividing by the unit vector again

items.prompt: A fictional microscope-stage log uses x positive right and y positive up. Move A is 15 units at 120 degrees clockwise from positive vertical; move B = (5,-4). A draft analysis treated A's angle as 120 degrees counterclockwise from positive horizontal. Correct that convention error, calculate R = A+B, find R's magnitude and standard direction counterclockwise from positive horizontal, and find its scalar projection along positive horizontal. Audit signs, units, and the triangle bound.

answer.correct: Because the angle is measured clockwise from positive vertical, A = (15sin120°,15cos120°) = (15sqrt(3)/2,-7.5), approximately (12.990,-7.5). R is approximately (17.990,-11.5). Its magnitude is approximately 21.352 units and atan2(-11.5,17.990) gives -32.59 degrees, equivalent to 327.41 degrees counterclockwise from positive horizontal. Projection along positive horizontal unit vector (1,0) is approximately 17.990 units. R is in quadrant IV, component and angle signs agree, units remain linear, and 21.352 is below 15 + sqrt(41), approximately 21.403, satisfying the triangle bound.
answer.summary: R approximately (17.990,-11.5), magnitude 21.352, direction 327.41 degrees, horizontal projection 17.990.
answer.reasoning: Translate the clockwise-from-positive-vertical convention into x = r sin(theta) and y = r cos(theta), add corresponding signed components, reconstruct with norm and atan2, and dot with (1,0).
acceptableResponses: Numerical values within rounding tolerance of 0.05 with the corrected angle convention, all requested interpretations, and checks
commonErrors: Using horizontal-axis cosine/sine ordering for A
Rejecting the equivalent negative atan2 angle instead of converting it to the requested 0-to-360-degree convention

criteria.description: Calculates the correct three signed components.
Interprets entries as components along declared axes.
Shows a valid sum-of-squares norm calculation and obtains magnitude 7.
Obtains the correctly signed unit vector.
Obtains both exact or accurate components.
Translates the clockwise-from-positive-vertical convention and resolves A with correct signs.
Checks signs, units, and triangle bound.

sources.title: NIST Digital Library of Mathematical Functions, Chapter 1, §§1.2(v) and 1.6(i)
creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.locator: §1.2(v), especially equations 1.2.38–1.2.52: vectors, scalar products, Euclidean norm, normalization, Cauchy–Schwarz equality, and triangle inequality; §1.6(i), especially equations 1.6.1–1.6.6: three-dimensional components, unit vectors, dot product, norm, and angle
sources.license: NIST public technical reference under NIST Copyrights & Disclaimers; third-party contributions and cited works excluded

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only standard vector definitions, relations, and inequalities are used. All explanations, contexts, examples, values, checks, and assessments are original.

agentAccess.notes: NIST rights and public robots routes were checked before DLMF access; no source-specific agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found.

claims.statement: Within this lesson's real Cartesian setting, a scalar is one real numerical value, whereas a vector is represented by an ordered component tuple and follows vector addition and scalar multiplication; the zero vector is valid but has no unique direction.
claims.scope: Real scalars and two- or three-dimensional Euclidean vectors in a declared Cartesian basis.
claims.uncertainty: More abstract vector spaces need not carry a Euclidean magnitude or geometric direction.

claims.statement: A vector can be expressed as a linear combination of coordinate unit vectors, and vector addition and scalar multiplication operate on the corresponding components.
claims.scope: Two- and three-dimensional Euclidean vectors in a declared orthonormal coordinate system.
claims.uncertainty: A component's sign and physical meaning depend on the chosen axes and orientation convention.

claims.statement: In an orthonormal Cartesian basis, a vector's magnitude is the square root of the sum of its squared components, and a nonzero vector divided by its magnitude is a unit vector.
claims.scope: Euclidean norm and unit vectors in the lesson's Cartesian examples.
claims.uncertainty: Other mathematical spaces can use different norms; the zero vector has no normalized direction.

claims.statement: For Euclidean vectors, the magnitude of a sum is at most the sum of the magnitudes; for two nonzero vectors equality occurs exactly when one is a positive scalar multiple of the other, and equality also holds if either vector is zero.
claims.scope: The Euclidean norm used throughout this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: The equality condition is stated for two vectors over the real numbers.

claims.statement: The dot product equals the product of vector magnitudes times the cosine of their included angle and can be used to test perpendicularity or calculate a scalar projection onto a unit direction.
claims.scope: Real Euclidean vectors and included angles from zero through pi radians.
claims.uncertainty: A zero vector has no direction, so an angle formula that divides by its magnitude is undefined.

claims.statement: For a two-dimensional vector whose direction angle is measured counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis, its components are magnitude times cosine and sine of that angle; quadrant-aware inverse tangent reconstructs the direction.
claims.scope: The lesson's explicitly stated two-dimensional coordinate convention.
claims.uncertainty: Fields using bearings or different reference axes require a different component convention; a one-argument arctangent alone loses quadrant information.

terms.definition: In this lesson, one real numerical value, which may be positive, zero, or negative and has no vector direction.

A mathematical object that supports vector addition and multiplication by scalars and can be represented by signed components in a chosen basis; only a nonzero Euclidean vector has a unique direction.
A signed scalar coefficient describing how much of a vector lies along one chosen coordinate direction.
The nonnegative length or Euclidean norm of a vector.
A vector of magnitude one used to specify a direction or basis axis.
A scalar formed by multiplying corresponding components and adding; it measures directional alignment.
The signed length of one vector along a unit direction, calculated with a dot product.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The vector-audit structure, route opening, directed-span and dot-product examples, component/magnitude misconception repair, angle-convention transfer, all values, feedback, recovery, and assessments are original. Every applied setting is fictional. The NIST source provides standard vector definitions, relations, and norm inequalities only. No source expression, diagram, table, or example is copied or adapted.

NIST rights and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical, navigation, or engineering advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
